source_metadata.title_raw: Jason S. Rosenberg, Esq. - A-19-075 - May 23, 2019 - Oakland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jason S. Rosenberg, Esq. 
City Attorney of South San Francisco 
Meyers Nave 
555 12th Street, Suite 1500 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-075 
Dear Mr. Rosenberg, 
May 23, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act") 1 on behalf of City of South San Francisco Councilmember Mark 
Nagales. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does Councilmember Nag_ales have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that 
precludes him from participating in decisions involving City-created tenant protection policies and 
programs based on his position as an outreach coordinator for the nonprofit organization, Project 
Sentinel? 
CONCLUSION 
No - it is not reasonably foreseeable that the City's tenant protection policy decisions will 
materially affect the Councilmember's employer. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-075 
Page No. 2 
South San Francisco is a general law city with a City Council. Councilmember Nagales is 
employed as an outreach coordinator for Project Sentinel, a nonprofit organization that advocates 
for housing-related issues. As part of its actions, the City Council may decide whether to implement 
certain tenant protection policies or programs, such as requiring relocation assistance, one-year 
lease minimums, and delayed rent increases. 
Project Sentinel is an Internal Revenue Code Section 50l(c)(3) nonprofit organization and 
U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development-approved Housing Counseling Agency 
whose purpose is to "develop and promote fairness and equality of housing for all persons and to 
advocate peaceful resolution of disputes for community welfare and harmony." The organization's 
functions include: 
• 
Conducting fair housing or discrimination "testing" investigations to determine whether a 
housing discrimination has occurred; 
• 
Providing landlord/tenant assistance and counseling, mortgage counseling, homebuyer 
education, community mediation and dispute resolution programs, and community outreach 
and education on fair housing, tenant rights, and landlord obligations; 
• 
Providing support to cities and counties in developing their "Analysis of Impediments to 
Fair Housing," and a wide array of technical assistance to property management 
professionals, owners, and local municipalities; and 
• 
Publishing a semi-weekly newspaper column, "Rent Watch," in major California 
newspapers, and operating a Housing Hotline. 
Project Sentinel has offices across the Bay Area and is funded through a variety of sources, 
including Community Development Block Grants I, federal grants, and donations. Most Project 
Sentinel programs are free, but certain programs charge a fee, such as First Time Home Buyer 
classes, pre-purchase counseling, and mortgage counseling. 
As an outreach coordinator, Councilmember Nagales is responsible for planning and 
implementing community outreach activities that will inform the community of available services 
and will establish ongoing working relationships with a wide spectrum of the community ranging 
from government officials, to other social service organizations and individual households. His 
duties include planning and conducting community outreach and education on landlord/tenant 
issues to promote agency services throughout San Mateo County; contacting residents, non-profit 
agencies and other community organizations to establish working relationship; conducting research 
and analysis to determine potential partners and cultivate those prospects that best align with 
Project Sentinel's landlord/tenant program priorities and goals; performing case management duties 
of housing counseling and dispute resolution; and other related duties as assigned. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-19-075 
Page No. 3 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. 
Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. Most pertinent 
to the analysis is the following: 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
As a paid employee of Project Sentinel, Councilmember Nagales has an interest in his 
employer as a source of income under the Act's conflict of interest provisions. 
Foreseeability: 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard for foreseeability 
differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Financial interests 
that are explicitly involved include an interest that is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
government d~cision. (Regulation 18701 ( a).) Under the facts you have provided, Project Sentinel 
would not be a named party in, or the subject of, any City Council decisions regarding tenant 
protection policies or programs. Thus, the effect on the nonprofit is reasonably foreseeable if it can 
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 
18701(b).) 
Materiality Standard- Financial Interest in a Source of Income: 
Regulation 18702.3 provides the standard for materiality of a source of income. (Regulation 
18702(a)(3).) The regulation indicates that an official's financial interest experiences a material 
effect when the source of the income is a nonprofit that will receive a measurable financial benefit 
or loss as a consequence of the decision at issue. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).) 
While Project Sentinel operates in the field of housing advocacy and education, any City 
Council decisions on tenant protection policies or programs would not be directly applicable to 
Project Sentinel. Project Sentinel's financial statement evidences that it receives the vast majority of 
its funding through governmental grants. Although, there could be overlap between the individuals 
who benefit from the tenant protection policies and programs and the individuals who utilize 
Project Sentinel's educational resources, there is no indication the decisions would affect the 
nonprofit's source funding nor that there would be other monetary effects on the nonprofit. 


File No. A-19-075 
Page No. 4 
Under the facts that you have provided, a decision by the City Council to implement tenant 
protection policies or programs will not give Project Sentinel a measurable financial benefit or loss 
because, as an organization, it will not be subject to those policies and programs designed to benefit 
individuals and families. Accordingly, it is not reasonably foreseeable that Project Sentinel will be 
materially affected by the decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~7o~ 
/4/ 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel Legal Division

Does Councilmember Nag_ales have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that 
precludes him from participating in decisions involving City-created tenant protection policies and 
programs based on his position as an outreach coordinator for the nonprofit organization, Project 
Sentinel?
sections.conclusion:
No - it is not reasonably foreseeable that the City's tenant protection policy decisions will 
materially affect the Councilmember's employer.

QUESTION: Does Councilmember Nag_ales have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act that 
precludes him from participating in decisions involving City-created tenant protection policies and 
programs based on his position as an outreach coordinator for the nonprofit organization, Project 
Sentinel?

CONCLUSION: No - it is not reasonably foreseeable that the City's tenant protection policy decisions will 
materially affect the Councilmember's employer.
